CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear logical progression from formalism to implications (additive compositionality, analogy explanations, and connection to SDR). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is good, with adequate related-work coverage; figures/tables aren’t detailed in the summary but examples and theory seem clearly presented. I note the authors explicitly call out unrealistic uniformity assumptions and computational limitations, which aids clarity, though some sections (e.g., practical implications and empirical validation details) could be expanded to make the presentation more accessible to applied readers like myself. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main theoretical contributions are stated early and carried through the methods and proofs with good writing quality. Notation appears consistent and well-defined for a mathematically oriented audience, and related work is adequately covered. One presentation weakness is that figures/tables and empirical examples are not detailed in the summary (making it unclear how effectively visual aids support the arguments), and the realistic limitations (e.g., Zipfian word frequencies) could be highlighted more prominently to guide readers through applicability of the theorems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear logical progression from formalism to implications (additive compositionality, analogy explanations, and connection to SDR). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is good, with adequate related-work coverage; figures/tables aren’t detailed in the summary but examples and theory seem clearly presented. I note the authors explicitly call out unrealistic uniformity assumptions and computational limitations, which aids clarity, though some sections (e.g., practical implications and empirical validation details) could be expanded to make the presentation more accessible to applied readers like myself.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main theoretical contributions are stated early and carried through the methods and proofs with good writing quality. Notation appears consistent and well-defined for a mathematically oriented audience, and related work is adequately covered. One presentation weakness is that figures/tables and empirical examples are not detailed in the summary (making it unclear how effectively visual aids support the arguments), and the realistic limitations (e.g., Zipfian word frequencies) could be highlighted more prominently to guide readers through applicability of the theorems.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers a clearly new theoretical perspective by formalizing compositionality for word vectors, proving additive compositionality (in a stronger distance sense) under explicit corpus assumptions, and deriving the correct operator when those assumptions fail; this goes beyond existing empirical observations about vector arithmetic. Its explicit connection between Skip‑Gram and the SDR framework also appears novel and nontrivial, differentiating the contribution from prior factorization and embedding analyses. I withhold a top score because the advances are primarily theoretical extensions of known models (Skip‑Gram, SDR) and hinge on strong corpus assumptions (noted as unrealistic), so while informative to experts, the novelty is notable rather than paradigm‑shifting. | B(4): The paper delivers a notable and non-trivial theoretical advance by formalizing compositionality of word vectors (including a stronger small-distance additive result under explicit corpus assumptions) and by deriving the correct composition operator when assumptions fail — this goes beyond typical empirical observations. Its explicit connection between Skip-Gram and the Sufficient Dimensionality Reduction framework, together with an information-theoretic optimality claim and explanation of why common Skip-Gram heuristics approximate SDR, represents a clear, novel perspective that differentiates it from prior work. The reliance on an unrealistic uniformity assumption and the computational cost of SDR temper the scope, so while quite informative to experts, it stops short of a transformative breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a clearly new theoretical perspective by formalizing compositionality for word vectors, proving additive compositionality (in a stronger distance sense) under explicit corpus assumptions, and deriving the correct operator when those assumptions fail; this goes beyond existing empirical observations about vector arithmetic. Its explicit connection between Skip‑Gram and the SDR framework also appears novel and nontrivial, differentiating the contribution from prior factorization and embedding analyses. I withhold a top score because the advances are primarily theoretical extensions of known models (Skip‑Gram, SDR) and hinge on strong corpus assumptions (noted as unrealistic), so while informative to experts, the novelty is notable rather than paradigm‑shifting.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper delivers a notable and non-trivial theoretical advance by formalizing compositionality of word vectors (including a stronger small-distance additive result under explicit corpus assumptions) and by deriving the correct composition operator when assumptions fail — this goes beyond typical empirical observations. Its explicit connection between Skip-Gram and the Sufficient Dimensionality Reduction framework, together with an information-theoretic optimality claim and explanation of why common Skip-Gram heuristics approximate SDR, represents a clear, novel perspective that differentiates it from prior work. The reliance on an unrealistic uniformity assumption and the computational cost of SDR temper the scope, so while quite informative to experts, it stops short of a transformative breakthrough.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important conceptual gap and offers a meaningful theoretical bridge between Skip‑Gram embeddings and the SDR framework, which could strongly influence future work. They also concur that impact is limited by strong/unrealistic corpus assumptions and lack of empirical validation or practical algorithms. Given these strengths and caveats, a score of 4 reflects substantial potential influence while acknowledging it is not yet fully transformative without follow‑up validation and algorithmic refinement.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important question—why additive composition and analogy arithmetic work for Skip‑Gram embeddings—and offers a formal justification plus a novel connection to the SDR framework, which could materially reframe theoretical understanding. However, the impact is limited by reliance on strong, partly unrealistic corpus assumptions (acknowledged Zipf mismatch) and a lack of reported empirical validation or experiments on standard benchmarks to demonstrate practical gains or sensitivity to assumptions. If the theoretical links are later empirically validated and approximations to SDR are made practical, the work could become influential; as presented it is a useful but not yet field‑changing contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important conceptual gap by giving a rigorous formalism for compositionality in word vectors and proving additive composition (in a stronger distance sense) under explicit corpus assumptions, which directly illuminates why analogical arithmetic works. Its theoretical bridge between Skip-Gram and the SDR framework — including an information-theoretic optimality claim and practical implications for approximation heuristics — can change how researchers view and justify embedding algorithms. The impact is somewhat tempered by reliance on an unrealistic uniformity assumption and computational expense of SDR learning, so while this work will strongly influence theory and open new research directions, it stops short of being fully transformative without further empirical/algorithmic follow-up.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes valuable, rigorous theoretical contributions connecting Skip‑Gram and SDR and shedding light on compositionality and analogy arithmetic. The primary concern is the strong corpus-generation/uniformity assumptions and lack of empirical validation; these limit immediate practical impact but do not undermine the theoretical novelty. Given its clear relevance to top venues but need for additional experiments or sensitivity analyses, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core topic for top NLP/ML venues (word embeddings, compositionality, and links to SDR) and offers potentially impactful theoretical connections (Skip‑Gram ↔ SDR and an explanation of analogies). However, from a pragmatic empirical standpoint it relies on strong corpus-generation assumptions (notably a uniformity assumption counter to Zipfian word frequencies) and lacks reported empirical validation, datasets, or downstream evaluations to substantiate practical relevance. Given the importance of empirical sensitivity analyses and baselines in this area, the work is borderline: of clear interest to the community but needing additional experiments/ablations to be a strong fit for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it provides a rigorous mathematical formalism for compositionality in word vectors, proves strong additive-compositionality results under explicit corpus-generation assumptions, and connects Skip-Gram to the SDR framework. The theoretical contributions (deriving correct composition operators when assumptions fail and explaining analogy arithmetic) target the community interested in foundations of representation learning, and the discussion of practical implications (relation to negative sampling, hierarchical softmax, GloVe approximations) situates it within venue expectations despite noted limitations like the unrealistic uniformity/Zipf issue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a rigorous theoretical contribution and candidly discusses limitations (e.g., unrealistic uniformity assumption, SDR cost) and situates itself in prior work. However, they also agree that empirical validation, datasets, evaluation metrics, ablations/sensitivity analyses, and reproducibility details are missing, leaving practical impact untested. Given the strong theory but absence of experimental support, a middle score reflects substantive intellectual contribution tempered by insufficient empirical substantiation.

reviewer_a.justification: From the provided summary the paper is largely theoretical and lacks empirical validation: datasets, evaluation metrics, and experimental comparisons are not specified, so claims of optimality and compositional gains are untested. Sensitivity analyses/ablations are missing despite reliance on strong modeling assumptions (the uniformity assumption is acknowledged as unrealistic given Zipfian word frequencies). Related work appears adequately covered and some limitations are discussed, but there is insufficient detail (code/hyperparameters) to reproduce or to assess practical impact, so the experimental depth is shallow.

reviewer_b.justification: From the summary the paper delivers a rigorous theoretical treatment and explicitly discusses limitations (e.g., unrealistic uniformity assumption, computational cost of SDR), and relates to prior work (connection to SDR, comparison to negative sampling/hierarchical softmax/GloVe). However, no experimental datasets, empirical evaluations, ablation/sensitivity analyses, or reproduction details (code, hyperparameters) are reported, leaving the empirical support and practical reproducibility weak. Given my emphasis on formal clarity, the strong theoretical contributions partially compensate, but the lack of experimental validation and reproducibility information prevents a higher score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides substantial formal derivations linking Skip‑Gram compositionality and SDR and that the proofs and corollaries are technically strong. However, they also concur that a key theorem relies on an unrealistic uniformity assumption and that the lack of empirical validation and discussion of SDR’s computational impracticality leave important gaps in applicability. Given solid theoretical contributions but significant untested and impractical assumptions, a middling score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear theoretical claims and provides formal proofs linking Skip-Gram compositionality and SDR, which is valuable; however, major assumptions (e.g., uniform corpus sampling) are acknowledged as unrealistic and are not empirically tested. There are no reported experiments, datasets, baselines, or metrics to validate that the theoretical operators or asserted ‘information-theoretic optimality’ translate to practical gains, and computational issues of SDR are not experimentally addressed. From a pragmatic empirical perspective this leaves the paper short on necessary sensitivity analyses and ablations to support robustness of its conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides formal proofs linking additive compositionality and Skip‑Gram to the SDR framework and derives the correct composition operator when assumptions fail, which indicates substantial theoretical support for its main claims. However, a key theorem depends on an unrealistic uniformity assumption (acknowledged by the authors) and the SDR learning algorithm’s computational impracticality are important qualifications that introduce logical gaps in applicability. Given these caveats but otherwise apparently complete derivations and the stated corollary explaining analogies, the work is technically strong though not flawless in practical realism.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a strong, principled theoretical comparison to relevant baselines (negative sampling, hierarchical softmax, GloVe) and meaningfully connects Skip‑Gram to the SDR framework. However, both note the absence of empirical evaluations, datasets, and metrics—plus limiting assumptions—that prevent a fully fair, practical comparison. Given the conceptual strength but missing experimental validation, a minor deduction is warranted, so I award a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper discusses and contrasts Skip-Gram with negative sampling, hierarchical softmax and GloVe and makes theoretical connections to SDR, so relevant methods are cited. However, no datasets, evaluation metrics, or empirical comparisons are specified, and there are no fair experimental conditions or sensitivity analyses to validate theoretical claims against benchmarks; the authors acknowledge some limitations (Zipf assumption, SDR cost) but do not empirically demonstrate when prior methods perform comparably or better. From a pragmatic empirical perspective this is therefore only a partial comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper situates itself against the right technical baselines (negative sampling, hierarchical softmax, GloVe) and provides a principled theoretical comparison by connecting Skip-Gram to the SDR framework and analysing when common heuristics are justified. However, the summary reports no empirical setup, shared datasets, or metrics to establish fairness of comparisons in practice, and the authors themselves note limiting assumptions (e.g., unrealistic uniformity) that weaken direct empirical comparability. Overall the comparisons are conceptually strong and the differences are clearly analyzed, but the lack of experimental parity or concrete empirical evaluation justifies a minor deduction.

main_claims: Provides a mathematical formalism for compositionality in word vectors and a theoretical justification for additive compositionality in Skip-Gram embeddings.

Proves that additive compositionality holds in a stronger sense (small distance rather than small angle) under certain assumptions on the corpus-generation process, and derives the correct composition operator when those assumptions fail.
Explains the success of vector arithmetic for solving word analogies as a corollary of the compositionality analysis.

Establishes a connection between the Skip-Gram model and the Sufficient Dimensionality Reduction (SDR) framework, showing Skip-Gram parameters can be modified to obtain SDR parameters and arguing that Skip-Gram learns information-theoretically optimal embeddings in the SDR sense; consequently, common Skip-Gram approximation heuristics can be used to approximate SDR models.
_summary.methodology: The paper performs a theoretical analysis proving properties of Skip-Gram embeddings under explicit assumptions on the corpus sampling process, derives composition operators (showing additive composition under uniformity assumptions), and proves equivalence/connection between Skip-Gram and the SDR factorization of Globerson and Tishby.
limitations: Theorem relying on a uniformity assumption is not realistic because word frequencies follow a Zipf distribution.
When the stated assumptions do not hold, compositionality is not additive and a different composition operator is required.
The original SDR learning algorithm is computationally expensive (involves information projections).

reviewer_a.name: Pragmatic Empiricalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with extensive experience deploying word embeddings and representation learning in applied systems and running large-scale empirical evaluations.
traits: Penalises papers that provide theoretical claims without empirical validation on at least two standard benchmarks (e.g., analogy tasks, downstream classifiers).
Requires explicit sensitivity analyses or ablations when results depend on strong modeling assumptions (e.g., corpus-generation process) and will downscore if assumptions are unrealistic or untested.
Prefers clarity on practical implications: demands demonstration that proposed composition operators yield measurable gains in real tasks or that theoretical improvements translate to practice.
Skeptical of claims of 'optimality' without empirical comparisons to standard baselines and will reduce score if theoretical optimality is not contextualized with real-world performance.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on practical significance and empirical evidence: accepts elegant theory only if accompanied by convincing empirical validation and realism of assumptions.

reviewer_b.name: Mathematical Formalist
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher in machine learning and theoretical NLP, with a track record of publishing rigorous analyses of representation learning models and probabilistic foundations.
traits: Rewards clear, novel theoretical formalizations and proofs that illuminate previously informal phenomena (e.g., compositionality of embeddings).
Values establishing formal connections between models and existing theoretical frameworks (e.g., linking Skip-Gram to SDR) and will upscore papers that bridge theory gaps.
Lenient about lack of extensive empirical work when the paper delivers substantial conceptual advances or resolves important theoretical questions.
Favors papers that identify precise conditions under which common heuristics are justified, even if those conditions are idealized.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates primarily on mathematical novelty, correctness, and conceptual clarity, tolerating limited experiments when theory provides strong explanatory power.
